name: gpt2

members.name: GPT2Config
members.signature: GPT2Config(vocab_size: int = 50257, hidden_size: int = 768, num_hidden_layers: int = 12, num_attention_heads: int = 12, intermediate_size: int = 3072, hidden_act: TextActivation = 'gelu_new', max_position_embeddings: int = 1024, pad_token_id: int | None = None, bos_token_id: int | None = 50256, eos_token_id: int | None = 50256, hidden_dropout: float = 0.1, attention_dropout: float = 0.1, initializer_range: float = 0.02, layer_norm_eps: float = 1e-05, tie_word_embeddings: bool = True, use_cache: bool = True, num_labels: int = 2, classifier_dropout: float | None = None, scale_residual_init: bool = True)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(vocab_size: int = 50257, hidden_size: int = 768, num_hidden_layers: int = 12, num_attention_heads: int = 12, intermediate_size: int = 3072, hidden_act: TextActivation = 'gelu_new', max_position_embeddings: int = 1024, pad_token_id: int | None = None, bos_token_id: int | None = 50256, eos_token_id: int | None = 50256, hidden_dropout: float = 0.1, attention_dropout: float = 0.1, initializer_range: float = 0.02, layer_norm_eps: float = 1e-05, tie_word_embeddings: bool = True, use_cache: bool = True, num_labels: int = 2, classifier_dropout: float | None = None, scale_residual_init: bool = True)

members.extended:
Defaults match the **124M-parameter** "small" checkpoint released in 2019
(the file historically known as `117M`).  Larger sizes are reached by
overriding `hidden_size` / `num_hidden_layers` / `num_attention_heads`
— see `lucid.models.text.gpt2._pretrained` for the canonical table.

members.name: GPT2DoubleHeadsModel

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: forward(input_ids: Tensor, mc_token_ids: Tensor, attention_mask: Tensor | None = None, labels: Tensor | None = None, mc_labels: Tensor | None = None)

members.summary: GPT-2 + LM head + multiple-choice classification head.
members.extended:
Identical contract to `GPTDoubleHeadsModel` but with GPT-2's
pre-LN trunk and `transformer.wte` parameter naming so checkpoints
port directly.  Used for tasks like RACE / Story Cloze where each
example presents a prompt followed by `C` candidate completions;
the model is jointly trained with the standard LM objective on each
choice and a `C`-way classification loss picking the correct one.

Inputs are 3-D: `input_ids` of shape `(N, C, L)` flattens to
`(N * C, L)` for the trunk and reshapes back to `(N, C, ...)` at
the heads.  `mc_token_ids` of shape `(N, C)` indexes the per-choice
pooling position (typically the trailing CLF token of each completion).

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
Hyperparameters.  `config.tie_word_embeddings` (default True)
ties the LM head weight to the `wte` embedding matrix.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.text.gpt2 import GPT2Config, GPT2DoubleHeadsModel
>>> cfg = GPT2Config(num_hidden_layers=2, hidden_size=64,
...                  num_attention_heads=2, intermediate_size=256)
>>> model = GPT2DoubleHeadsModel(cfg).eval()
>>> input_ids = lucid.tensor([[[15496, 11, 995, 13],
...                            [15496, 11, 1820, 13]]])    # (N=1, C=2, L=4)
>>> mc_ids = lucid.tensor([[3, 3]]).long()
>>> out = model(input_ids, mc_token_ids=mc_ids)
>>> out.mc_logits.shape   # (N=1, C=2)
(1, 2)
notes:
Reference: Radford et al., *"Language Models are Unsupervised
Multitask Learners"*, OpenAI Technical Report, 2019; multiple-choice
head follows the GPT-1 fine-tuning recipe (Radford 2018 §3.3).

The combined loss when both objectives are supplied is



$$
\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{joint}}
    = \mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{CLM}} + \mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{MC}},
$$

where $\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{MC}}$ is a softmax cross-entropy
over the `C` candidate completions.

attributes.name: lm_head
attributes.description: Tied output projection of shape `(hidden_size, vocab_size)`.

attributes.name: mc_head
attributes.description:
Per-choice pooled-token scalar projection of shape
`(hidden_size, 1)`.

members.name: GPT2DoubleHeadsOutput
members.signature: GPT2DoubleHeadsOutput(lm_logits: Tensor, mc_logits: Tensor, loss: Tensor | None = None, lm_loss: Tensor | None = None, mc_loss: Tensor | None = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(lm_logits: Tensor, mc_logits: Tensor, loss: Tensor | None = None, lm_loss: Tensor | None = None, mc_loss: Tensor | None = None)

members.extended:
Aggregates the per-choice causal-LM logits, the per-choice multiple-
choice classification logits, and the optional per-objective and
combined losses produced when training Radford-style multiple-choice
fine-tunes (Story Cloze, RACE).

parameters.name: lm_logits
parameters.description:
LM head logits of shape `(N, C, L, vocab_size)` — one
distribution per token of every batch x choice pair.

parameters.name: mc_logits
parameters.description:
Multiple-choice head logits of shape `(N, C)` — one scalar per
candidate completion.

parameters.name: loss
parameters.description:
Sum of `lm_loss` and `mc_loss` when both are available;
otherwise the single available loss, or `None`.

parameters.name: lm_loss
parameters.description: Shift-cross-entropy on the LM head when `labels` is supplied.

parameters.name: mc_loss
parameters.description:
Cross-entropy on the multiple-choice head when `mc_labels` is
supplied.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.text.gpt2 import GPT2Config, GPT2DoubleHeadsModel
>>> cfg = GPT2Config(num_hidden_layers=2, hidden_size=64,
...                  num_attention_heads=2, intermediate_size=256)
>>> model = GPT2DoubleHeadsModel(cfg).eval()
>>> input_ids = lucid.tensor([[[15496, 11, 995, 13]]])    # (N=1, C=1, L=4)
>>> mc_ids = lucid.tensor([[3]]).long()
>>> out = model(input_ids, mc_token_ids=mc_ids)
>>> out.lm_logits.shape, out.mc_logits.shape
((1, 1, 4, 50257), (1, 1))
notes:
Reference: Radford et al., *"Language Models are Unsupervised
Multitask Learners"*, OpenAI Technical Report, 2019.

members.name: GPT2ForSequenceClassification

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: forward(input_ids: Tensor, attention_mask: Tensor | None = None, labels: Tensor | None = None)

members.summary: GPT-2 with a last-token sequence-classification head.
members.extended:
Wraps `GPT2Model` with a dropout-regularised linear classifier
operating on the **rightmost non-pad token's** hidden state — the
canonical decoder-style pooling.  Unlike BERT (CLS at position 0),
decoder LMs only have a meaningful summary at the *last* attended
position since every preceding position has attended to a strict
prefix.

parameters.name: config

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.text.gpt2 import GPT2Config, GPT2ForSequenceClassification
>>> cfg = GPT2Config(num_labels=3, num_hidden_layers=2, hidden_size=128,
...                  num_attention_heads=2, intermediate_size=512)
>>> model = GPT2ForSequenceClassification(cfg).eval()
>>> input_ids = lucid.tensor([[15496, 11, 995, 13]])
>>> out = model(input_ids)
>>> out.logits.shape   # (B=1, num_labels=3)
(1, 3)
notes:
Reference: Radford et al., *"Language Models are Unsupervised
Multitask Learners"*, OpenAI Technical Report, 2019.

Pooling picks index $t^\star = \sum_t \mathbf{1}[\mathrm{mask}_t] - 1$ (the rightmost real token) per row, then computes



$$
z = W_{\mathrm{cls}}\, \mathrm{Dropout}(h_{t^\star}).
$$

When `labels` is supplied, cross-entropy over `num_labels` classes
is computed and exposed as `output.loss`.

attributes.name: dropout
attributes.description: Dropout layer applied to the pooled last-token embedding.

attributes.name: classifier
attributes.description:
Final linear of shape `(hidden_size, num_labels)` producing
per-class logits.

members.name: GPT2LMHeadModel

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: forward(input_ids: Tensor, attention_mask: Tensor | None = None, labels: Tensor | None = None)

members.summary: GPT-2 with a tied causal-language-modeling head.
members.extended:
Wraps `GPT2Model` with an output linear projection whose weight
matrix is bound to the input `wte` embedding table when
`config.tie_word_embeddings` is `True` — halving the parameter cost
of the softmax layer.  This is the entry point for
`lucid.models.GenerationMixin.generate` and is the
standard recipe for both pre-training and downstream generative use.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
Hyperparameters.  `config.tie_word_embeddings` (default True)
controls whether the LM-head weight is shared with the input
embedding matrix.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.text.gpt2 import GPT2Config, GPT2LMHeadModel
>>> cfg = GPT2Config(num_hidden_layers=2, hidden_size=128,
...                  num_attention_heads=2, intermediate_size=512)
>>> model = GPT2LMHeadModel(cfg).eval()
>>> input_ids = lucid.tensor([[15496, 11, 995, 13]])
>>> out = model(input_ids)
>>> out.logits.shape   # (B=1, T=4, V=50257)
(1, 4, 50257)
notes:
Reference: Radford, Wu, Child, Luan, Amodei, and Sutskever, *"Language
Models are Unsupervised Multitask Learners"*, OpenAI Technical Report,
2019.

The training loss (when `labels` is supplied) is the next-token
shifted causal-LM cross-entropy:



$$
\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{CLM}}
    = -\frac{1}{B(T-1)} \sum_{b, t}
      \log p_\theta(y_{b, t+1} \mid x_{b, \le t}),
$$

with positions labelled `-100` excluded.

attributes.name: lm_head
attributes.description:
Output projection of shape `(hidden_size, vocab_size)` mapping
each token's hidden state to vocabulary logits.

members.name: GPT2Model

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: forward(input_ids: Tensor, attention_mask: Tensor | None = None)

members.summary: GPT-2 decoder trunk — N pre-LN transformer blocks topped by `ln_f`.
members.extended:
Implements the modified architecture of Radford, Wu, Child, Luan, Amodei,
and Sutskever, 2019.  Differs from GPT-1 in three ways: (i) **pre-LN
blocks** — LayerNorm is applied to the residual *input* of each
sub-layer rather than after the residual add, which stabilises
optimisation past depth $L \geq 24$; (ii) a **final LayerNorm**
(`ln_f`) caps the trunk; (iii) residual-output projection weights are
initialised with an extra $1/\sqrt{2N}$ factor (Radford 2019
§2.3) to keep activation variance roughly invariant to depth.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
Hyperparameters controlling vocabulary, depth, width, head count,
and the residual-init scaling toggle.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.text.gpt2 import GPT2Config, GPT2Model
>>> cfg = GPT2Config(num_hidden_layers=2, hidden_size=128,
...                  num_attention_heads=2, intermediate_size=512)
>>> model = GPT2Model(cfg).eval()
>>> input_ids = lucid.tensor([[15496, 11, 995, 13]])   # "Hello, world."
>>> out = model(input_ids)
>>> out.last_hidden_state.shape   # (B=1, T=4, H=128)
(1, 4, 128)
notes:
Reference: Radford, Wu, Child, Luan, Amodei, and Sutskever, *"Language
Models are Unsupervised Multitask Learners"*, OpenAI Technical Report,
2019.

Each pre-LN block computes



$$
\begin{aligned}
    x &\leftarrow x + \mathrm{Attn}(\mathrm{LN}_1(x)) \\
    x &\leftarrow x + \mathrm{MLP}(\mathrm{LN}_2(x))
\end{aligned}
$$

so the residual stream carries un-normalised state.  The trunk produces
final hidden states $H \in \mathbb{R}^{B \times T \times H}$
which the LM head projects to vocabulary logits.

attributes.name: wte
attributes.description:
Word / token embedding table of shape `(vocab_size, hidden_size)`.
HuggingFace name retained verbatim for state-dict portability.

attributes.name: wpe
attributes.description:
Learned absolute position embedding of shape
`(max_position_embeddings, hidden_size)`.

attributes.name: ln_f
attributes.description: Final LayerNorm applied to the trunk output.

attributes.name: config_class
attributes.description: Registry pointer for matching-config instantiation from disk.

attributes.name: base_model_prefix
attributes.description:
State-dict prefix (`"transformer"`) under which the trunk is
nested in task-head variants.

members.name: GPT2Tokenizer
members.signature: GPT2Tokenizer(vocab: dict[str, int], merges: list[tuple[str, str]], add_prefix_space: bool = False, normalizer: Normalizer | None = None, special_tokens: SpecialTokens | None = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(vocab: dict[str, int], merges: list[tuple[str, str]], add_prefix_space: bool = False, normalizer: Normalizer | None = None, special_tokens: SpecialTokens | None = None)

members.extended:
A thin convenience subclass of
`lucid.utils.tokenizer.ByteLevelBPETokenizer` with
`<|endoftext|>` pre-registered as the bos / eos / unk marker.

parameters.name: vocab
parameters.description: Token-string → id map (byte-mapped Unicode keys).

parameters.name: merges
parameters.description: Ordered BPE merges; index = rank.

parameters.name: add_prefix_space
parameters.description:
Prepend a space so the first word receives the `Ġ` marker.
Off in GPT-2 (matches the published vocab); on in RoBERTa.

parameters.name: normalizer
parameters.description: Default `lucid.utils.tokenizer.normalizers.NFC`.

parameters.name: special_tokens
parameters.description:
Override the default GPT-2 registry (single `<|endoftext|>`
shared across bos / eos / unk).

members.name: GPT2TokenizerFast
members.signature: GPT2TokenizerFast(vocab: dict[str, int], merges: list[tuple[str, str]], add_prefix_space: bool = False, normalizer: Normalizer | None = None, special_tokens: SpecialTokens | None = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(vocab: dict[str, int], merges: list[tuple[str, str]], add_prefix_space: bool = False, normalizer: Normalizer | None = None, special_tokens: SpecialTokens | None = None)

members.extended:
Bit-identical to `GPT2Tokenizer`.  The BPE merge loop runs
in C++ via the engine-side `BPE` binding for production
throughput.

Constructor parameters mirror `GPT2Tokenizer` — see that
class for the full reference.

members.name: gpt2_large
members.signature: gpt2_large(pretrained: bool | str = False, weights: GPT2LargeWeights | None = None, overrides: object = {})
members.summary: Construct a GPT-2 large (774M) decoder trunk.
members.extended:
Third of the four canonical OpenAI variants: $L=36$ transformer
blocks, $H=1280$ hidden, $A=20$ attention heads,
intermediate width 5120.  Roughly 774M parameters — released in
August 2019 after the staged-release decision; SOTA zero-shot on
LAMBADA at release time.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description: Reserved for future weight registration; currently a no-op.

parameters.name: weights
parameters.description: Explicit weights enum member; takes precedence over `pretrained`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Optional `GPT2Config` field overrides forwarded into the
underlying config.

returns.description:
Decoder trunk configured with the GPT-2 large size and any
overrides.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.text.gpt2 import gpt2_large
>>> model = gpt2_large().eval()
>>> input_ids = lucid.tensor([[15496, 11, 995, 13]])
>>> out = model(input_ids)
>>> out.last_hidden_state.shape   # (1, 4, 1280)
(1, 4, 1280)
notes:
Reference: Radford et al., *"Language Models are Unsupervised
Multitask Learners"*, OpenAI Technical Report, 2019.

members.name: gpt2_large_lm
members.signature: gpt2_large_lm(pretrained: bool | str = False, weights: GPT2LargeLMWeights | None = None, overrides: object = {})
members.summary: Construct a GPT-2 large (774M) model with the tied causal-LM head.
members.extended:
Same trunk as `gpt2_large` (L=36, H=1280, A=20, ~774M parameters),
wrapped with the tied LM head for pre-training and free-form generation.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description: Reserved for future weight registration; currently a no-op.

parameters.name: weights
parameters.description: Explicit weights enum member; takes precedence over `pretrained`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Optional `GPT2Config` field overrides forwarded into the
underlying config.

returns.description: GPT-2 large trunk wrapped with the tied LM head.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.text.gpt2 import gpt2_large_lm
>>> model = gpt2_large_lm().eval()
>>> input_ids = lucid.tensor([[15496, 11, 995, 13]])
>>> out = model(input_ids)
>>> out.logits.shape   # (1, 4, 50257)
(1, 4, 50257)
notes:
Reference: Radford et al., *"Language Models are Unsupervised
Multitask Learners"*, OpenAI Technical Report, 2019.

members.name: gpt2_medium
members.signature: gpt2_medium(pretrained: bool | str = False, weights: GPT2MediumWeights | None = None, overrides: object = {})
members.summary: Construct a GPT-2 medium (355M) decoder trunk.
members.extended:
Second of the four canonical OpenAI variants: $L=24$ transformer
blocks, $H=1024$ hidden, $A=16$ attention heads,
intermediate width 4096.  Roughly 355M parameters — the inflection
point at which zero-shot performance on reading comprehension /
summarisation begins to substantially outperform random baselines
(Radford 2019 Table 3).

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description: Reserved for future weight registration; currently a no-op.

parameters.name: weights
parameters.description: Explicit weights enum member; takes precedence over `pretrained`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Optional `GPT2Config` field overrides forwarded into the
underlying config.

returns.description:
Decoder trunk configured with the GPT-2 medium size and any
overrides.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.text.gpt2 import gpt2_medium
>>> model = gpt2_medium().eval()
>>> input_ids = lucid.tensor([[15496, 11, 995, 13]])
>>> out = model(input_ids)
>>> out.last_hidden_state.shape   # (1, 4, 1024)
(1, 4, 1024)
notes:
Reference: Radford et al., *"Language Models are Unsupervised
Multitask Learners"*, OpenAI Technical Report, 2019.

members.signature: gpt2_medium_lm(pretrained: bool | str = False, weights: GPT2MediumLMWeights | None = None, overrides: object = {})

members.extended:
Same trunk as `gpt2_medium` (L=24, H=1024, A=16, ~355M parameters),
wrapped with the tied LM head for pre-training and free-form generation.

parameters.description: Reserved for future weight registration; currently a no-op.

members.name: gpt2_small
members.signature: gpt2_small(pretrained: bool | str = False, weights: GPT2SmallWeights | None = None, overrides: object = {})
members.summary: Construct a GPT-2 small (124M) decoder trunk.
members.extended:
Smallest of the four canonical OpenAI variants from Radford, Wu, Child,
Luan, Amodei, and Sutskever, 2019: $L=12$ transformer blocks,
$H=768$ hidden, $A=12$ attention heads, intermediate width
3072.  Roughly 124M parameters (historically released as the "117M"
file before a re-count).  This is the default entry point for GPT-2
research and the parameter-count match for GPT-1.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description: Reserved for future weight registration; currently a no-op.

parameters.name: weights
parameters.description: Explicit weights enum member; takes precedence over `pretrained`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Optional `GPT2Config` field overrides forwarded into the
underlying config.

returns.description:
Decoder trunk configured with the GPT-2 small size and any
overrides.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.text.gpt2 import gpt2_small
>>> model = gpt2_small().eval()
>>> input_ids = lucid.tensor([[15496, 11, 995, 13]])   # "Hello, world."
>>> out = model(input_ids)
>>> out.last_hidden_state.shape   # (1, 4, 768)
(1, 4, 768)
notes:
Reference: Radford, Wu, Child, Luan, Amodei, and Sutskever,
*"Language Models are Unsupervised Multitask Learners"*, OpenAI
Technical Report, 2019.

members.name: gpt2_small_cls
members.signature: gpt2_small_cls(pretrained: bool | str = False, weights: GPT2SmallWeights | None = None, overrides: object = {})
members.summary: Construct a GPT-2 small (124M) model with a sequence-classification head.
members.extended:
Same trunk as `gpt2_small` (L=12, H=768, A=12), augmented with a
last-token linear classifier of output width `config.num_labels`.
The canonical recipe for fine-tuning GPT-2 on GLUE-style sentence
classification tasks when decoder-style features are preferred to
bidirectional ones.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Encoder-weight selector.  `False` → fully random init; `True`
→ loads the pretrained `gpt2_small` decoder trunk
(`GPT2SmallWeights.DEFAULT`) into the `.transformer`
submodule; a tag string selects a specific encoder checkpoint.
**The classifier head is always randomly initialised** (fine-tuning
starting point — no GLUE-fine-tuned head ships).

parameters.name: weights
parameters.description:
Explicit encoder-weights enum member; takes precedence over
`pretrained`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Optional `GPT2Config` field overrides forwarded into the
underlying config.  Pass `num_labels=N` to set the number of
classes (default 2).  Overrides that change the trunk shape are
incompatible with loading pretrained encoder weights.

returns.description:
GPT-2 small trunk wrapped with the last-token classifier head
(encoder pretrained when requested; head random).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.text.gpt2 import gpt2_small_cls
>>> model = gpt2_small_cls(num_labels=3).eval()
>>> input_ids = lucid.tensor([[15496, 11, 995, 13]])
>>> out = model(input_ids)
>>> out.logits.shape   # (1, num_labels=3)
(1, 3)
notes:
Reference: Radford et al., *"Language Models are Unsupervised
Multitask Learners"*, OpenAI Technical Report, 2019.

members.name: gpt2_small_lm
members.signature: gpt2_small_lm(pretrained: bool | str = False, weights: GPT2SmallLMWeights | None = None, overrides: object = {})
members.summary: Construct a GPT-2 small (124M) model with the tied causal-LM head.
members.extended:
Same trunk as `gpt2_small` (L=12, H=768, A=12, ~124M parameters),
wrapped with the tied LM head used for pre-training and free-form
generation via `lucid.models.GenerationMixin.generate`.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description: Reserved for future weight registration; currently a no-op.

parameters.name: weights
parameters.description: Explicit weights enum member; takes precedence over `pretrained`.

parameters.name: **overrides

Optional `GPT2Config` field overrides forwarded into the
underlying config.

returns.description: GPT-2 small trunk wrapped with the tied LM head.

examples:

>>> model = gpt2_small_lm().eval()
>>> input_ids = lucid.tensor([[15496, 11, 995, 13]])
>>> out = model(input_ids)
>>> out.logits.shape   # (1, 4, 50257)
(1, 4, 50257)
notes:
Reference: Radford et al., *"Language Models are Unsupervised
Multitask Learners"*, OpenAI Technical Report, 2019.

members.name: gpt2_xlarge
members.signature: gpt2_xlarge(pretrained: bool | str = False, weights: GPT2XLargeWeights | None = None, overrides: object = {})
members.summary: Construct a GPT-2 XL (1.5B) decoder trunk.
members.extended:
Largest of the four canonical OpenAI variants: $L=48$ transformer
blocks, $H=1600$ hidden, $A=25$ attention heads,
intermediate width 6400.  Roughly 1.5B parameters — fully released in
November 2019 after staged disclosure; remained the largest publicly
available autoregressive LM until GPT-3 (Brown 2020).

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description: Reserved for future weight registration; currently a no-op.

parameters.name: weights
parameters.description: Explicit weights enum member; takes precedence over `pretrained`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Optional `GPT2Config` field overrides forwarded into the
underlying config.

returns.description: Decoder trunk configured with the GPT-2 XL size and any overrides.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.text.gpt2 import gpt2_xlarge
>>> model = gpt2_xlarge().eval()
>>> input_ids = lucid.tensor([[15496, 11, 995, 13]])
>>> out = model(input_ids)
>>> out.last_hidden_state.shape   # (1, 4, 1600)
(1, 4, 1600)
notes:
Reference: Radford et al., *"Language Models are Unsupervised
Multitask Learners"*, OpenAI Technical Report, 2019.

members.name: gpt2_xlarge_lm
members.signature: gpt2_xlarge_lm(pretrained: bool | str = False, weights: GPT2XLargeLMWeights | None = None, overrides: object = {})
members.summary: Construct a GPT-2 XL (1.5B) model with the tied causal-LM head.
members.extended:
Same trunk as `gpt2_xlarge` (L=48, H=1600, A=25, ~1.5B parameters),
wrapped with the tied LM head for pre-training and free-form generation.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description: Reserved for future weight registration; currently a no-op.

parameters.name: weights
parameters.description: Explicit weights enum member; takes precedence over `pretrained`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Optional `GPT2Config` field overrides forwarded into the
underlying config.

returns.description: GPT-2 XL trunk wrapped with the tied LM head.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.text.gpt2 import gpt2_xlarge_lm
>>> model = gpt2_xlarge_lm().eval()
>>> input_ids = lucid.tensor([[15496, 11, 995, 13]])
>>> out = model(input_ids)
>>> out.logits.shape   # (1, 4, 50257)
(1, 4, 50257)
notes:
Reference: Radford et al., *"Language Models are Unsupervised
Multitask Learners"*, OpenAI Technical Report, 2019.

summary: GPT-2 family — Radford et al., 2019 (pre-LN decoder-only transformer).
citation: Radford, Alec, et al. "Language Models are Unsupervised Multitask Learners." OpenAI Technical Report, 2019.
theory:
GPT-2 retains GPT-1's decoder-only causal-LM objective



$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{LM}}(\theta)
    = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \log p_\theta(x_t \mid x_{<t}),
$$

but introduces three architectural refinements that have since
become standard for autoregressive transformers.  **Pre-LayerNorm**
moves the LayerNorm to the input of every sub-block (attention and
MLP), so each block computes $h \leftarrow h + f(\mathrm{LN}(h))$
instead of $h \leftarrow \mathrm{LN}(h + f(h))$; combined with
a **final LayerNorm** (`ln_f`) after the last block, this yields
much more stable optimisation at depth.  Residual projection weights
are initialised with an extra $1/\sqrt{2N}$ factor (where
$N$ is the layer count), which keeps activation variance
roughly invariant to depth.

The tokenizer switches to a byte-level BPE with a 50257-symbol
vocabulary (50256 merges plus the `<|endoftext|>` sentinel), and
context length doubles from 512 to 1024.  The released family spans
four sizes — *small* (124M, the defaults above), *medium* (355M),
*large* (774M), and *xl* (1.5B) — by scaling
$(\text{hidden\_size}, \text{num\_hidden\_layers}, \text{num\_attention\_heads})$ while holding `intermediate_size =
4 \cdot \text{hidden\_size}` and head-dim = 64 fixed.

The paper's headline finding is that a sufficiently large LM trained
on WebText (~40 GB scraped from outbound Reddit links) performs
competitive **zero-shot** transfer across reading comprehension,
translation, summarisation, and QA — without any task-specific
fine-tuning.  This is the empirical seed of in-context learning and
the modern prompting paradigm.
